item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
First image is owned by Brian Hamilton, under All Rights Reserved.

Second image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0

Both images have since been removed.

Maybe I am a bit influenced by being a biotechnologist irl, but in reality I work in genetics, not microbiology.
Yet, seeing such a good usage of scientifical terminology is a pleasure in this kind of SCPs.

Upvoted because it works, and the incident log works. But the "Redacted Syndrome" in the last part didn't age that good.
I really would like to have the "[REDACTED]" being limited and reworked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sublimates, not evaporates.
+1 for being intensely disturbing.

item.title: Mod Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Yui907, you're responding to a comment from almost five and a half years ago.

May I do it for you?

Contact Decibelles from the Rewrite Team, (if she hasn't seen this and responded to it).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally I think a non-static article would be nice. Or some kind of coding to change the dates as time goes on.

Anyway, the dates are still not redacted.

May I do it for you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvote for redaction in Containment Procedures. Otherwise, this is interesting, but would probably draw a no-vote from me, as it doesn't "hook" me very well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I added the full names of bacteria to the article because that's how it's done in the literature. Also, I suspect it will make more sense to the reader to know that "S. mitis" is, in fact, "Streptococcus mitis".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one, but I have two quips.

Firstly, there shouldn't be anything redacted, expunged or otherwise removed from the containment procedure… if that part of the procedure is unknown, it can't be performed.

Secondly, the containment procedures seem overly detailed. I think we can assume that standard bio-material access restrictions/protocols apply, including quarantine procedures, except perhaps for the addition of O5 approval. HAZMAT containment is fairly well understood, especially by The Foundation, so enumerating everything probably isn't necessary.

I really like the idea that it isn't infectious itself, but that it causes infections. Clever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've noticed that there are alot of pathogenic SCP's and this one is especially interesting. I know that cross testing SCP's can be very unsuccessful but I've read some pretty good ones. For instance, SCP-187. There was an experiment log where she was placed in the prescence of other SCP's and that opened up some pretty interesting ideas about the other SCP's.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the research team was attempting to contain [DATA EXPUNGED] in a class-██ SCP-stable pressure cylinder, which led to [REDACTED] combining with [REDACTED]. A full molecular biological analysis of this is available in [REDACTED].

Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only quip with this would be the one date blacked out and the other (same date again?) not blacked out. Does blacking out the other count as a minor edit?

Still, language used is impressive, shows high level knowledge of the subject. Had to resort to the dictionary several times. All in all, very nice work :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i wholeheartedly approve of the level of scientific detail involved in this article

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know about you, but shitting out my digestive system would not be funny.

Upvoted. I suggest reading a study guide for the AP Biology Exam if you have no clue what anything means of this page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In which lethal farts are no laughing matter. (OK, maybe a little bit of laughing, because I'm juvenile that way.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just saw some gas canisters on a truck today and though "hey, that would be a cool basis for an SCP."

I then promptly forgot about it until I saw this.

I am officially blaming you for stealing my not-even-half-formed idea (even though you probably posted this before I saw the canisters). Jerk.

But yeah, very well done, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, I was just chastised in chat.

I will probably redact the dates in the current Operation report and close it into an Incident, and just add the remaining incidents that are in my brain as I have time to write them over the next couple days.

(grumble grumble would still prefer it to be nonstatic for a while grumble grumble senior staff grumble)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: NO! That's a bad researcher, NO! That is NOT how it's done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, I intend to keep updating for a while as "new" events occur.

My thinking is, we have lots of articles involving resolved events and SCPs under essentially static conditions. I'd like to try to do something different there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: have to say, don't really like the idea of a "current" item log, especially if it has the potential to become very long awhile still being date-relevant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree that this is better done than many previous supergerm creators, which is probably a good thing, since I'm pretty sure the last two or three that got posted were deleted shortly after.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one. As long as it's better and different enough than others done before I have no problem with it.

That being said, i can't for the life of me remember the specifics of the last mutagen. Other than the Universal Carcinogen girl blob thing I can't think of any current ones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While disease modifier has been done before, this is interesting and different enough to warrant an upvote. Also, the backstory is intriguing.
